data.title: AMD May Have More Upside Than Nvidia (Upgrade)
data.description:
As the rotation out of tech names gained momentum at the start of the year, I downgraded my rating on Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) earlier this year. I'm glad I did so, because the stock was pretty much flat since my last coverage.

I now upgrade back to a strong buy, as I see strong signs that the rotation out of tech is reversing its course. Leaving the macro risks on the side (a topic I discussed in this coverage), I think AMD could see a strong second half of the year.

In fact, I argue that the upside is higher for AMD than for Nvidia, at least from a price action perspective.

In this piece, I discuss my view on the main pillar of the bull case on AMD: its pivot from selling individual components to rack-scale systems.

There is still a long uphill road ahead until AMD takes market share from Nvidia. However, to me, it's a matter of when, not if. In my view, the "when" is likely the back half of the year, with Q4 more likely than Q3.

I upgrade to a strong buy, and I expect to see new highs before the end of the year.

From Accelerators to Rack-Scale Systems

The title above represents what I believe to be the main pillar of the bull case on AMD.

The company is pivoting from selling individual components like the Instinct MI-3xx/-4xx series GPUs and the EPYC series CPUs to a full-stack offering. In fact, this pivot is the specific reason they acquired ZT Systems for $4.9B last year.

This is exactly what the incumbent leader, Nvidia, is doing with its GB300 NVL72 rack-scale system. On top of selling the Blackwell GPUs (there are 72x GPUs in the NVL72 configuration) and the Grace CPUs (36x), the company is also offering interconnect, networking, systems, and software.

Let me put into perspective now how important these ancillary products are in the AI data center buildout. In its last fiscal year (FY 2026), Nvidia reported $31.38B in data center networking revenue.

That's almost 2x AMD's data center revenue in FY 2025 ($16.6B).

So, networking alone is bringing Nvidia almost twice as much revenue as AMD's total data center segment. In fact, I strongly believe that Nvidia's turnkey full rack-scale AI systems are what made it gain market share in the GPU space so quickly.

It's not that people want to buy GPUs alone. They buy them via rack-scale systems, and there are 72x in each NVL72 setup. That's why Nvidia has a 90% share in the data center compute market.

How did I come up with that figure?

AMD's Q4 2025 data center revenue was $5.4B. NVIDIA's data center revenue in Q4 FY26 was $51.3B. That's a total market of $56.7B. As a side note, Broadcom doesn't make GPUs, so I'm not counting its ASIC revenue here. As for Intel, well, I'm going to leave Intel on the sidelines, given that its Gaudi 3 data center GPU is not widely used by hyperscalers and its next-gen GPU, Crescent Island, is not expected until H2 2026 (customer sampling, not ramp).

Back to AMD, its data center market share is barely in the low teens. Therefore, I was very pleased when AMD showcased the Helios rack-scale system in October last year. Helios integrates Instinct GPUs, EPYC CPUs, and Pensando networking. As for the software, Helios is designed to work with the ROCm, which has a limited adoption when compared to Nvidia's CUDA software stack.

That said, AMD's software stack didn't deter the likes of OpenAI, Meta, or Oracle. The 6 GW OpenAI deal, worth tens of billions of dollars in annual revenue, was signed in October 2025 and requires the first 1 GW of capacity to be deployed starting in H2 2026. The other 6 GW deal with Meta contains a similar timeline, with the first GW deployment expected to begin in H2 2026.

The last important one is the expansion with Oracle, which includes 50,000 AMD Instinct MI450 GPUs starting in Q3 2026.

Well, there is actually a fourth one, after the company expanded its collaboration with Tata Consultancy Services in February this year, for 200 MW.

What I find encouraging is the customer traction as soon as the company announced its Helios rack-scale system.

Given the momentum in the AI data center buildout, I wouldn't be surprised to see more partnerships with the other hyperscalers this year. So what?

Unlike the case for Nvidia (whose GPUs are present in pretty much any serious data center in the U.S.), these potential partnerships are not priced in for AMD.

In my view, AMD is the underdog here, and this presents optionality if the company is able to steal market share from the incumbent. That's why, once the war and inflation fears are over, I feel more bullish on AMD than Nvidia.

Risks

As I've shown above, AMD's market share in the data center compute segment is barely in the low teens. Therefore, even though the pivot toward rack-scale systems is encouraging, there is a long uphill road ahead.

The latest update in the Celestica partnership is that Helios will be customer ready in late 2026. It's still in the second half of the year, although I would have preferred to see it in early H2 rather than late H2.

Why?

Because growth is not particularly exciting right now. Management guided Q1 2026 revenue to about $9.8 billion, which is down about 5% sequentially from the Q4 2025 level.

In fact, the data center strength in Q4 last year looks less impressive if you strip out the China contribution. Specifically, without $390 million of China MI308 sales, AMD's Q4 data center revenue would have been $4.99 billion, below the $5.07 billion analysts expected.

I warn readers that volatility remains on the table until the company is able to recognize sales for its Helios systems.

Looking Ahead

The stock is trading at 30x forward earnings. That's more expensive than Nvidia's 21x multiple or Broadcom's 27x forward P/E.

This is a compliment for AMD, by the way.

Unlike Nvidia, AMD's valuation didn't compress as much in the tech selloff since October last year. In fact, even after Huang's $1T upgrade (from $500B initially) in sales potential for Nvidia's Blackwell and Rubin AI chips through 2027, the stock has barely reacted. In other words, the market is complacent with Nvidia's outstanding results.

In my view, that's not the case for AMD, as noted by the high single-digit spike on February 24, after announcing the Meta deal.

Overall, I upgrade AMD to a strong buy, and I expect shares to retest new highs in the back half of the year.

data.title: Why Annaly Capital Is My Favorite 'End Of The World' Hedge
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

Sing with me:

"It's the end of the world as we know it. It's the end of the world as we know it. It's the end of the world as we know it. . . And I feel fine!" – R.E.M.

We often get wrapped up in what is happening in the world, and more specifically, in how the world is changing. Today's world is unrecognizable from the world my grandfather grew up in.

In World War II, it was an amazing technological advancement for people to be able to go to the movie theater to get news updates on the war. These were generalized updates covering the major events.

Fast-forward to today, and there are updates on every individual attack. We can pull up videos of almost everything that happens and even track the movements of warships at a level that WW2 generals could only dream about having in a classified briefing.

As with all technology, it comes with its positives and negatives. It's good to be informed and know what is going on; it is rather unhealthy to sit there refreshing your page every 15 minutes to read about the next back-and-forth.

Added to the speed at which information is relayed around the world today is the speed at which sometimes convincing videos can be created from nothing using AI. When you watch a video, you don't really know whether to believe your own eyes or not.

We walk around in a world where we can access an enormous amount of information from our phones, which are rarely out of arm's reach. Unfortunately, we are also in a world where the quality of that information is highly questionable.

Is it the "end of the world"? As we know it—yes. The world is changing, and with it comes turmoil in the markets. Yet as the world changes, there will be some things that will always be the same.

Some investors will be gamblers, looking to make quick flips based on being faster to react to news than others. We see the oil markets gyrating in response to every attempted strike on oil-producing assets. Other investors will be long-term investors who buy and hold through the turmoil, with the conviction that the investments they hold will produce a good long-term return.

Today, we want to take a look at an investment that has gone through several major changes in the world.

Annaly Capital Management – Yield 13.5%

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) is the oldest mortgage REIT that focuses on agency MBS. It pioneered the investment strategies that its peers have followed. What makes Agency MBS an interesting investment is its ability to outperform during recessions.

Here is a look at NLY before and after the dot-com bust:

Note that it was underperforming materially from IPO until 2001, then it skyrocketed during the recession from 2001 to 2004.

Similarly, it declined and underperformed from 2004 to 2006, but then rallied as recession risk increased and had strong results through the GFC:

As we sit here today, NLY has underperformed the past five years, but is starting its rally up, having bottomed in late 2023.

Despite its price only being up 18%, NLY's total return has kept pace with the S&P 500 as its dividend remains robust. We have not seen the crossing point where the S&P 500 declines and NLY runs ahead, but that possibility is becoming increasingly probable.

Outperforming During Recessions

Why does NLY outperform during recessions? Quite simply, because NLY is invested in an asset class that has minimal credit risk. Agency MBS are mortgage-backed securities that hold mortgages guaranteed by the "agencies" Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. If a borrower defaults, the agency buys back the mortgage at par. As a result, materially all the credit risk is carried by the agency, not by the investors in agency MBS.

Agency MBS tends to have prices that correlate strongly with US Treasuries. They are often used as an alternative to US Treasuries for institutions that have high liquidity demands, like banks or insurance companies.

By holding an asset class that takes on minimal credit risk, NLY tends to see its book value improve in times when investors are looking for safety.

That isn't to say NLY avoids all risk. NLY avoids credit risk, which is one type of risk. The type of risk that NLY takes on is interest rate risk.

Profiting From Interest Rate Spreads

NLY's strategy is to borrow short-term money and hold Agency MBS, which typically has an expected life of about seven years. While mortgages are 30-year obligations, they amortize, which means principal is paid down every month, and they can be prepaid at any time. As a result, borrowers frequently pay off a mortgage early by making larger payments, selling their house, or refinancing.

NLY borrows in the "repo" markets, which are typically 30-90 day borrowings that are secured by the agency MBS they hold. The interest rate is usually slightly higher than the Federal Reserve Target Rate.

So, NLY is borrowing short-term and holding long-term assets. It profits from the difference. As a result, NLY's historical trading price has been highly correlated with how wide the spread is between the 10-year Treasury and 2-year Treasury.

When the United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) is significantly higher than the United States 2-Year Bond Yield (US2Y), NLY's price tends to rise. When the 10-year is below the 2-year Treasury, NLY's price tends to bottom. NLY outperforms so much during recessions because that's a period where the Treasury curve tends to steepen, and it steepens because we see the Federal Reserve slash rates.

The flip side is that NLY will struggle the most when the Fed is hiking short-term rates and short-term rates are lower than long-term rates. This is usually during periods of economic expansion.

NLY has been keeping pace with the S&P 500 (SP500) the past three years, even though we haven't seen a recession, and even though the Fed has been cutting rates at a very slow pace. Simply the end of the Fed hiking cycle and the inversion of the yield curve have been enough for NLY to post strong numbers and see the start of the recovery. History tells us that it's just getting started, and if we see a recession, NLY is one of the few stocks capable of being truly countercyclical and rising while the rest of the market falls.

Conclusion

It is the "end of the world as we know it" for various reasons. In the 2000s, the world as we knew it was ending. Computers have revolutionized so much in our lives that today it is difficult for many of us to imagine living without them. Overall, most of us would consider that a positive, as we live a much higher standard of living, and many of us can trace our labor to the internet.

There were definitely some negatives as well. Technology can be used for good or bad. We use technology to heal the sick, while simultaneously using it to wage war on each other. AI will be the same. It will be used for great things and for bad things. There is simply no way around that.

As investors, we need investments that we know can be bedrocks through challenging periods—that will step up and outperform during periods that are difficult.

NLY will not be the best holding in your portfolio all the time. Their earnings and, therefore, their dividend will fluctuate with the interest rate curve. Yet, when recession strikes and uncertainty rises, it is one of those holdings that can really shine when fear is at its highest.

While others are singing about the end of the world, we will feel fine.

data.title: DiDi Global: Waiting For International Segment To Show Earnings Growth (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on DiDi Global Inc. (DIDIY) was a buy rating because all the growth levers were firing up, and I expected earnings growth to accelerate. Fast forward to today; I think the operating story is still intact, but the earnings story is not as clean anymore. Revenue and transactions were strong, and China continues to execute well. The issue is that International, while growing very quickly, saw losses widen sharply due to heavier reinvestment. That makes the timing of earnings acceleration harder to underwrite, which is why I am moving to a neutral view.

4Q25 results update

DIDIY reported a mixed quarter. While revenue was up 10.5% y/y, core platform transactions were up ~14% y/y, core platform GTV was up ~20% y/y, and platform sales from the China Mobility and International segments were up ~25% y/y, margins and profits were materially weaker. Cost of revenues increased 9.2% y/y, and operations and support expenses increased 15.5% y/y. The sales and marketing expense increase was the significant one, up 95% y/y. Other expenses like R&D were also up 17.3% y/y, and G&A expenses were up 27.7% y/y. Ultimately, the operating loss widened to RMB 2.7 billion, a big step up from RMB 0.2 billion.

China is still the core growth engine

I have no complaints about the China business; it is doing absolutely great. DIDIY managed to continue driving growth via better monetization and delivered higher segment profit. For reference, China Mobility has seen twelve consecutive quarters of double-digit growth, and in the latest quarter, average daily transactions reached a record 38.9 million. Coupled with the numbers I noted above, this is a pretty clean set of numbers. It is not as if DIDIY benefited from any one-off tailwind as well. Management said China GTV growth was mainly driven by more transactions, and platform sales growth was driven by GTV growth and improved operating efficiency. Then, they went on to say that adj. EBITA grew because of higher platform sales, albeit partly offset by higher consumer incentives. All of this indicates that DIDIY is benefiting from the flywheel effect of more rides, more platform revenue, and more profits.

Looking ahead, I would expect the China segment to continue compounding growth at the current rate. GTV probably continues to grow at high teens to low 20s percent, as management said it plans to keep investing in 2026 by raising driver engagement and offering more affordable product choices to consumers (this should drive up volume).

International is why the rating is neutral

It is also fair to say that International was the strongest growth driver for this quarter, but I think it is also the key reason why I downgraded to neutral. Here are some numbers for reference: transactions grew 24.5% y/y, driving GTV up 47.1% y/y, and that led to Platform Sales being up 22.3% y/y to RMB2.9 billion. Those are very strong top-line numbers, and since management said the GTV growth was mainly driven by higher transaction volume, demand is clearly there. But note that the segment adj. EBITA loss widened by a significant amount, up RMB2.7 billion y/y to RMB3.4 billion. This is not the type of organic growth that I like to see, and management confirmed that the wider loss was directly tied to higher spending on incentives and marketing. I have previously touched on the bull case: if DIDIY succeeds in pulling this off, it would have significant scale, and once it hits critical mass adoption, profits will start to flow in.

The good news is that management said international mobility demonstrated sustained profitability for the second consecutive year. He also said the recent step up in strategic investment in new overseas businesses in Q4 saw growth that exceeded expectations and that it was seeing synergies being realized across multiple business lines in Latin America. The problem is that management did not show how DiDi got from a profitable international mobility business to a reported international segment that still lost RMB3.4 billion in Q4. In simple terms, they did not show how much of the reported international segment loss came from the newer overseas businesses they are investing in and how much came from the core international mobility business itself (sustained profitability doesn’t mean profitability went up; it could have been diluted). I think this matters a lot for the equity story because if the core mobility business is truly profitable, then those losses may be more about growth investments, which investors can be more patient with if they believe scale will drive future earnings. But if profitability at the core is being diluted at the same time, then the earnings path is not that clean, and this uncertainty gives investors less reason to give management the benefit of the doubt.

For what it’s worth, I can see the upside case. Overseas growth is strong, management sounds confident, and they said that the underlying mobility operation is profitable. But the losses were real, and management did not frame them as a one-quarter issue that should quickly reverse (i.e., it could get worse). The spending was noted as a strategic investment, and management said it has plans to continue investing in 2026. Given the lack of certainty in the path to earnings growth accelerating, I think it’s best I sit on the sidelines for now.

Valuation

The earnings story is no longer as clean as I thought. At its core, the China growth story is still great, but the step up in reinvestment is likely to continue weighing on the near-term numbers. From a valuation perspective, this is very worrisome because it means the timeline for earnings growth to accelerate gets pushed out further. While revenue growth may accelerate, this is a question mark for now. Consensus has also cut its near-term estimates by a huge ton (as you can see from the chart above). The one-year forward PE multiple is no longer relevant given negative earnings, so the right metric is the two-year forward (more normalized), which is now at 13x. The problem with this is that it implies reinvestments stop, and DIDIY starts letting revenue flow through to earnings. Given I have no confidence in when this reinvestment cycle will stop, I am refraining from making a judgment on what multiple deserves to trade at today. For this reason, I downgrade to hold.

Conclusion

My revised view for DIDIY is now a hold rating. I still like the underlying demand story. China remains strong, and there is a credible upside case if the international scale eventually drives more profits. But I do think the recent Q4 quarter put a dent in the equity story because of the step-up in international losses, together with management’s plan to keep investing, which means the path to earnings growth is less visible than I previously thought. Until I get better clarity on that trade-off, I think staying on the sidelines is the right call.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nationwide Loomis Short Term Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

The bond market delivered a solid total return with low volatility in the fourth quarter, closing out a generally positive year for the asset class. Fixed-income assets remained supported by a backdrop of slow but positive economic growth, an annualized inflation rate that largely held below 3%, and accommodative US Federal Reserve (Fed) policy. The Fed enacted two-quarter point interest rate cuts and announced the end of its multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Additionally, investors appeared to anticipate that the Fed was likely to continue easing in 2026. These developments, in combination, fueled positive returns across all major segments of the market.

Performance Review

The Fund underperformed its benchmark during the quarter, 1.06% (IS share class) vs. 1.16%. An overweight allocation to High Yield Corporates was additive to performance, as was Investment Grade Corporates. A US Treasury underweight was a drag on performance for the quarter.

Overall, strategy risks for the Fund declined during the quarter. Corporate bond risk declined slightly and remains near 50% of risk budget. While spreads widened during the quarter they remain narrow and the investment grade risk premium is also at the low end of the range. Lack of bottom-up opportunities heading into year-end provided the team room to reduce risk. Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) risk continues to be allocated in the lower half of our risk budget. Asset-Backed Securities (ABS) risk remained steady, using about one-third of risk budget.

Outlook

Fixed income markets delivered a solid positive total return as rates declined while the curve steepened on the back of two 25 basis points (bps) Federal Reserve (Fed) cuts during the fourth quarter. Risk premiums, or credit spreads, drifted slightly higher but remained near historic tights. The Federal Reserve continues to grapple with their dual mandate as inflation remains elevated while labor market conditions have deteriorated. The lack of official data due to the extended government shutdown made policy decisions more challenging for the Federal Reserve and there was an increase in voting member dissents over the last two meetings. The Fed Funds Rate target now stands at 3.75%. Markets are currently pricing in only a 50% probability of another cut in the first quarter of 2026, although just over two more cuts are priced by year end 2026. Spread sectors remained well supported, with new supply easily absorbed.

Corporate fundamentals remain at a healthy level by historical standards. Growth in the first quarter of 2026 may receive a boost through higher tax refunds and a reversal of the negative impacts from the shutdown. However, weak corporate pricing power and rising debt service costs suggest margin compression going forward. On the consumer front, we believe that moderating demand for labor, the continued decline in excess savings, and increasing impatience with higher prices are potential catalysts for weaker spending, which may be further exacerbated by higher prices for goods.

We continue to believe that we are in the late expansion phase of the credit cycle, with growth slowing to below trend, while inflation - which is still above target, should moderate as the effects of tariffs dissipate. While we do not anticipate that the US will enter a recession, we continue to see elevated risk of a downturn scenario. We are concerned that slowing rates of hiring and reduced affordability may weigh more heavily on incomes and consumption. Additionally, the risk of additional Federal Government stimulus leading up to mid-term elections may also add to the growing fiscal burden. This concern has been evident in the still elevated level of term premium in the longer end of the yield curve. Market volatility may remain elevated due to ongoing geopolitical tensions and interventionism.

We continue to favor spread sectors, such as corporate bonds and securitized assets. The Fund's corporate bond risk relative to benchmark decreased slightly over the quarter but remains around 50% of risk budget. The team continues to hold a small number of high yield issuers we believe offer value.

We believe asset-backed securities (ABS) continue to be a favorable alternative to front end US Treasurys. We generally favor consumer-related collateral and prefer the top part of the capital stack but are not limited to the highest quality band of the capital structure. Currently, the Fund's ABS risk relative to benchmark is around one-third of risk budget.

The Fund continues to hold commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), with the current benchmark relative risk in the lower half of the risk budget. When opportunities arise within CMBS, we tend to favor senior parts of the capital stack. We continue to follow our process of seeking to build diversified portfolio while favoring asset classes that we believe can generate security selection.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Iran War Creates Earnings Upside
data.description:
Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) is a leading upstream-focused energy enterprise with a growing production base that is set to profit handsomely in the first quarter from surging price realizations for its petroleum products. The most recent escalation in the Middle East between the U.S. and Israel on one side and Iran on the other has led to an explosion of petroleum prices above $100 per barrel, which is set to provide a significant boost to Exxon Mobil’s earnings and free cash flow prospects.

I calculate that the energy company, based on Q4'25 production volumes, could generate ~$4.5B in incremental net income for the first quarter, assuming that oil prices remain above $100 per barrel until the end of the month. With EPS estimates also set for major upside revisions in the weeks ahead, I am going all-in before the firm's Q1'26 earnings report, despite shares trading at fresh 52-week highs.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Exxon Mobil a strong buy in my last article after the company's fourth-quarter earnings report card was released, mainly because I liked the energy firm’s main focus on key growth production plays such as Permian and Guyana, which significantly propelled Exxon Mobil’s earnings growth in the upstream segment. With the recent outbreak of hostile activities in the Middle East, Exxon Mobil has a strong catalyst for higher earnings in the near and medium term and could potentially accelerate its capital returns as a result.

New Iran conflict sets stage for massive earnings and FCF upswing

A new war in the Middle East erupted at the end of February, after the U.S. and Israel attacked Iranian military installations, causing a significant spike in petroleum prices. The price for WTI crude currently sits at $101 per barrel, which indicates that Exxon Mobil is set for a material earnings boost in the first quarter. In Q4'25, Exxon Mobil reported an average price realization in its U.S. upstream business of $58.57 per barrel, so petroleum prices right now are about 72% (+$42 per barrel) higher than they were last quarter.

Prices only rose this significantly in the month of March, so Exxon Mobil obviously did not benefit from higher petroleum prices during the entire quarter, but the earnings impact is likely going to be material nonetheless: Exxon Mobil had a daily production volume in the fourth quarter of ~3.53 million barrels per day.

A ~$42 per barrel price delta for Exxon Mobil implies daily incremental earnings potential of ~$150M. Assuming that oil prices will remain at around $100 (or slightly higher) until the end of the month, Exxon Mobil could be on track to achieve ~$4.5B in incremental earnings in Q1'26. This $4.5B in additional earnings from the company's upstream activities translates to about $1.08 in incremental per-share earnings for the first quarter.

Most importantly, since Exxon Mobil is making a strategic pivot in terms of boosting high-potential production plays in its upstream segment, like Permian or Guyana, a higher-for-longer price environment would obviously significantly enhance Exxon Mobil’s earnings profile long term as well: according to Exxon Mobil's last investor update, the company has guided for approximately 65% of its 2030 projected production to come from high-growth, so-called 'advantaged assets.'

The rise in petroleum prices is, in my opinion, going to lead to a serious upside revision trend ahead of the firm's earnings report on April 2, 2026. So far, the company has seen 7 EPS estimate upside revisions in the last 90 days, but with petroleum prices exploding in the wake of the Iran war, chances are that the market is going to see more upside EPS revisions in the next several weeks.

Exxon Mobil’s valuation

Exxon Mobil has seen a significant uptrend lately, as the energy producer's shares responded favorably to a changing price picture in the petroleum market. Shares of Exxon Mobil are currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18.4X, which is above the historical 3-year average of 15.1X. Chevron, a key competitor in the upstream segment, is trading at a higher 21.4X earnings multiplier, in part because the firm is growing its production base more aggressively than Exxon Mobil, following the Hess acquisition in 2025.

However, petroleum prices are at their highest since 2022, following the onset of the Russia-Ukraine conflict, and I believe energy companies have upside left, especially if higher petroleum prices lead to an acceleration of capital return plans (stock buybacks) for Exxon Mobil.

If Exxon Mobil revalues to a COP-like P/E ratio, which is entirely possible if the conflict in the Middle East drags on, shares of the upstream-heavy energy enterprise could have a fair value in the neighborhood of $186 per share (+16% upside revaluation potential).

Risks with Exxon Mobil

The biggest risk for Exxon Mobil is a material (20%+) contraction in petroleum prices in a short period of time. This would most likely severely damage my bullish upside case for shares of Exxon Mobil and result in a compressing valuation factor for XOM. To make such a scenario a reality, the U.S., Israel, and Iran would have to strike a credible ceasefire agreement to calm investors and energy markets. Recently, the U.S. said it would delay strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure for a 5-day period for the U.S. and Iran to resume negotiations. A successful resolution of hostilities would likely lead to a significant decline in price realizations for Exxon Mobil.

Final thoughts

Exxon Mobil is seeing EPS estimates that are getting revised to the upside, and more revisions are likely as we approach the first-quarter earnings date, in my opinion. Exxon Mobil's first quarter earnings will clearly be under the impression of surging petroleum prices, which are set to add significantly to the company's bottom line: I expect to see ~4.5B in incremental net profits in the first quarter, or about $1.08 in additional earnings per share.

While Exxon Mobil's shares have already responded to the Middle East developments, I believe the risk profile here is very attractive, especially with the energy enterprise strategically pivoting into high-growth upstream production plays, such as the Permian and Guyana. A higher-for-longer price environment also implies potential for accelerating capital returns, which would make shares more attractive to investors, despite a higher price tag.

data.title: Dow Jones: Watch The 46,710 Resistance
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The price actions of the US Wall Street 30 CFD index (a proxy of the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA)) have tumbled as expected and broken below the key 200-day moving average on Wednesday, March 18, 2026.

On Monday, March 23, 2026, the US Wall Street 30 CFD index extended its bearish move to print an intraday low of 45,213 seen during the London session. All in all, it has plummeted by 10% from its current all-time high printed on February 10, 2026 to Monday’s March 23, 2026 low, reinforced by a flattening of the US Treasury yield curve triggered by rising stagflation risk due to global oil supply shock arising from the US-Iran war.

Risk-on behaviour roared back on Monday, March 23, 2026, after US President Trump sent a social media message that planned strikes against Iran’s energy infrastructure will be paused for five days as both sides are engaged in a renewed negotiation process, despite Iran's repeated assertion that no direct negotiations have been held with the US.

The TACO regime, the popular acronym, “Trump Always Chickens Out,” has its footprints in the global financial markets yesterday, where market participants remembered the ex-post “Liberation Day” events in late April 2025 where Trump walked back on his aggressive tariffs and paused the US’s trade war 2.0 with China, inducing a V-shaped recovery in global stock markets.

Last year’s April “Liberation Day” TACO regime was a reaction to a sell-off in risk assets caused by “words” rather than actions, which are military strikes on stakeholders’ physical infrastructure in the current context, and in turn, are likely to have lasting economic damages that cannot be easily reversed by a change of rhetoric from Trump.

Hence, Monday’s rally in risk assets is likely a fake head, also known as a dead cat bounce.

Intermarket analysis and technical analysis suggest that the medium-term V-shaped rally for the US stock market and global equities in general remains elusive now.

The VIX/MOVE ratio has not reached an extreme level on the upside

The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) is the implied volatility of the S&P 500, a gauge for US equities. On the other hand, the ICE BofA MOVE Index (MOVE) measures the implied volatility of US Treasuries.

Based on the latest price action of the VIX/MOVE ratio as of Tuesday, March 24, 2026, at the time of writing, it is trading below its 20-day moving average with a series of “lower highs and lower lows,” which suggests bond (US Treasuries) volatility is dominating, which implies interest rate uncertainty is the core driver at this juncture, and equity volatility is likely not fully pricing in such macro risk yet (may lead to more potential downside for US stock indices) (see Fig. 1).

Also, the VIX/MOVE ratio has not crossed above its daily Bollinger Bands’ upper limit, where such movements in the past led to or coincided with significant bullish reversals in the S&P 500 on November 20, 2025, October 16, 2025, and April 8, 2025 (see Fig. 1).

Let's now decipher the short-term trajectory (1-3 days) of the US Wall Street 30 CFD index and its supporting elements from a technical analysis perspective.

Dow Jones - Bearish reaction at 200-day moving average

Watch the 46,710 key short-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 45,237/190 may expose the next intermediate supports at 44,975/810 and 44,505 (see Fig. 2).

On the other hand, a clearance above 46,710 invalidates the bearish reversal scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the next intermediate resistances at 47,338 and 47,923.

Key elements to support the bearish bias on Dow Jones (DJIA)

  • Yesterday’s rally stalled at the 200-day moving average and the upper boundary of the descending channel from the February 26, 2026 high.
  • The hourly MACD trend indicator staged a bearish reaction at its horizontal resistance level.

data.title: PGIM Short Duration Muni Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Recap

For the quarter, the PGIM Short Duration Muni Fund Class Z returned 0.80% net of fees and outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg 1-8 Year Municipal Index, gross of fees.

Key Contributors

  • The Fund's yield curve positioning.
  • Security selection in Public Power.

Key Detractors

  • An overweight to Pre-Pay Gas bonds.

Market Review

In Q4, total return performance for tax-exempt municipal bonds was positive, as issuance remained heavy and the Fed resumed its rate-cutting policy with a 25-bp cut in both October and December. While bonds on the long end of the muni curve outperformed short and intermediate bonds during the quarter, longer maturities underperformed short maturities as the muni curve steepened over the full year.

The tax-exempt market, as represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, returned 1.56% in Q4 (on a total return basis), while the Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Index posted total returns of 1.37%. Municipal/Treasury yield ratios on 5-year, 10-year, and 30-year maturities ended the period at 65% (+3 pp), 66% (-4 pp), and 88% (-2 pp), respectively.

Gross issuance in December totaled $41 billion and net issuance totaled $10 billion, bringing gross and net issuance for 2025 to $582 billion and $237 billion, respectively. Taxable issuance was $2 billion, representing 6% of December's total gross supply.

On the demand side, tax-exempt mutual bond funds posted around $4 billion in inflows in December, driven primarily by inflows from national funds, ETFs, and intermediate funds. For 2025, inflows totaled around $49 billion ($14 billion for mutual funds and $35 billion for ETFs). Redemptions in December totaled $31 billion, lower than the historical average for the month.

Quarter

The Fund's curve flattener position contributed to performance as the 2s10s portion of the muni curve flattened.

The Fund's duration positioning had a negligible impact on performance.

Barrons: PGIM Investments ranked 4 out of 48, 8 out of 47, 12 out of 46 firms for the 1-, 5- and 10-year periods ended 12/31/2024, respectively. See back page for methodology which takes into account Lipper rankings. PGIM Short Duration Muni Fund (Class Z) Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-year periods as of 12/31/2024 for the Short-Intermediate Municipal Debt Funds category were: 19/79, 42/77, 47/75, and 4/59, respectively. Lipper Funds category rankings are based on total return, do not take sales charges into account, and are calculated against all funds in each fund's respective Lipper category. Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2025: 24/85, 12/76, 38/76, and 6/62, respectively. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

The Fund's security selection in the Public Power sector contributed to performance. An overweight position in Tobacco also contributed to performance. An overweight to Pre-Pay Gas detracted from performance as spreads widened.

Portfolio Outlook

We enter 2026 with a very steep 10s/30s tax-exempt munis curve and reasonable long-end yield ratios. All-in yields are still very attractive, with the YTW on the Index around 2011 levels. In addition, taxable equivalent yields (TEY) are competitive relative to corporates. IG corporate spreads have tightened more than IG muni spreads, and HY muni spreads have widened. If IG corporate spreads widen, relative value may be challenged. As long as the economy remains resilient, we view the notable dispersion in HY muni performance as an opportunity to select credits with attractive relative value compared to corporates.

In terms of fundamentals, although state revenue growth is slowing (0.6% growth expected in 2026), so is state spending (median 0.2% growth expected in 2026). With Gross Domestic Product (GDP) forecasted to grow at trend, we expect many revenue bond sectors (e.g., transportation, development, and dedicated tax bonds) will perform well. In addition, rainy day funds remain healthy and estimated expenses for 2026 have declined from the record reached in 2024 of 14.9%. While the upgrade/downgrade ratio has moderated to 1.1x, most sectors have held up well compared to higher education (0.7x) and charter schools (0.7x).

On the technical front, a pull-forward in issuance prompted by uncertainty, as seen in 2025 due to tax legislation or political scrutiny of vulnerable sectors (e.g., universities with cloudy fiscal outlooks), is not expected to be repeated in 2026. That stated, we expect supply to be flat YoY, with ~$600 billion in gross supply and $100-$200 billion of net supply anticipated. While the estimated net supply is elevated, inflows are sufficient to support muni valuations.

In taxable munis, rate volatility has returned to pre-liftoff levels, opening the door for more volatility going forward. Record IG corporate issuance in 2026 may also put pressure on taxable spreads.

We continue to implement barbell positioning. We have more conviction in the 1-3 year and 15-20 year portions of the muni curve, with most of our overweight allocation focused on the back end of the curve given its steepness. This overweight has been tempered to address the potential for an “Overheating” scenario, which could cause the long end to selloff. We favor prepaid gas despite the negative technical, student loans, planned amortization class (PAC) bonds, municipal utility district (MUD) bond anticipation notes (BANs), along with Puerto Rico, some tobacco, and multi-family housing bonds. We are cautious on healthcare, universities, and local government bonds (e.g., NYC and Chicago).

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: FCC Router Ban Plugs Into Gains
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

U.S. bans new foreign-made routers over security risks. (00:14) SK hynix (HXSCL) to buy $7.9B equipment from ASML. (01:20) Puig (PUIGF) stock jumps after Estée Lauder (EL) confirms merger talks. (01:57)

This is an abridged transcript.

Netgear (NTGR) is on our biggest movers list. NTGR is up 14%.

Shares surged after the Federal Communications Commission added foreign-made Wi-Fi routers to its “Covered List,” effectively banning the import of new models on national security grounds.

The FCC said in a statement that the foreign-made routers introduce “a supply chain vulnerability that could disrupt the U.S. economy, critical infrastructure, and national defense” and pose “a severe cybersecurity risk that could be leveraged to immediately and severely disrupt US critical infrastructure.”

The move targets future devices while allowing existing inventory and installed routers to remain in use.

The impact will extend beyond Chinese players such as TP-Link, affecting U.S.-headquartered firms as well.

Companies including Netgear (NTGR), Eero (AMZN), and Google (GOOG) Nest design their products domestically but depend heavily on manufacturing bases in Asia.

SK hynix (HXSCL) is acquiring advanced production equipment valued at 12 trillion won ($7.9 billion) from Dutch company ASML (ASML), aiming to meet rising demand for memory chips.

The South Korean chipmaker announced the decision through a regulatory filing. Reports say the extreme ultraviolet scanners will be delivered by December 2027 for its next-generation production processes.

The move comes amid SK Hynix's push to expand the production of memory chips, including high bandwidth memory, on the back of soaring demand from the artificial intelligence industry.

According to a separate report, SK Hynix (HXSCL) is seeking to raise 10 trillion won to 15 trillion won ($10 billion) from a potential listing in the US, the Korea Economic Daily reported.

The company is currently the main HBM supplier to Nvidia (NVDA), although Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and Micron (MU) supply it with smaller volumes.

Shares of Spanish beauty group Puig (PUIGF) jumped on Tuesday after Estée Lauder (EL) confirmed it is in talks about merging the two companies.

Puig (PUIGF), which is behind brands such as Rabanne and Charlotte Tilbury, has a market cap of EU8.8 billion ($10.3B). Estee Lauder has a market value of $31B.

Puig’s Spain-listed shares jumped as much as 15% on Tuesday, while Estée Lauder (EL) was up 0.6% in U.S. premarket trading.

Key Takeaways

  • Markets: 2025 marked the third consecutive year of this AI-driven cycle. The market cap-weighted S&P 500 Index rose 17.9% in the year, while the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index gained just 11.4%. The Magnificent Seven, this market's nucleus, rose 24.9% in 2025. While performance comparisons will vary based on the time period, cap-weighted and equal-weighted indexes have produced similar returns over long periods of time.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in consumer staples, a health care overweight and IT underweight proved beneficial.
  • Detractors: Quarterly underperformance was driven primarily by stock selection in the communication services, IT, materials and real estate sectors.
  • Outlook: We anticipate the AI debate will carry over in 2026, and we will continue to participate in AI in a measured and disciplined way. Yet, as other investors continue to myopically focus on AI, we expect to find additional idiosyncratic opportunities in overlooked corners of the market. We believe these seeds will bear fruit in the years to come.

Performance Review

  • The S&P 500 managed a 2.7% gain in the fourth quarter, while the Strategy captured roughly half of that.
  • On the plus side, consumer staples holdings such as Nestle (NSRGF), Coca-Cola (KO) and Unilever (UL) and a health care overweight shined as concerns of a tech bubble grew.
  • On the downside, we are underweight Alphabet (GOOGL) and Apple (AAPL), two Magnificent Seven stocks that performed well, and overweight Oracle (ORCL), which gave back some of its large gains from earlier in 2025. Increasing competitive dynamics in the wireless industry also weighed on T-Mobile (TMUS).
  • 2025 marked the third consecutive year of this AI-driven cycle. The market cap-weighted S&P 500 Index rose 17.9% in the year, while the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index gained just 11.4%. The Magnificent Seven, this market's nucleus, rose 24.9% in 2025. Since ChatGPT launched three years ago, the cap-weighted S&P 500 has delivered nearly twice the gains of its equal-weighted peer. The Magnificent Seven, meanwhile, has surged a staggering 332%.
  • While performance comparisons will vary based on the time period, cap-weighted and equal-weighted indexes have produced similar returns over long periods of time.
  • In 2025, and throughout the last three years, the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy has enjoyed healthy gains, in part due to its investments in AI participants like Alphabet, Broadcom, Meta Platforms, Microsoft and Oracle.
  • We have not gained as much as the cap-weighted S&P 500, our benchmark, however, as we maintain diversification to prevent the portfolio from becoming overly concentrated.
  • Market conditions in 2025 provided opportunities in companies we have long followed and admired such as: Automatic Data Processing (ADP)(a fourth-quarter addition), Inditex, L3Harris (LHX), Marsh & McLennan (MRSH), Old Dominion Freight Lines (ODFL) and TE Connectivity (TEL). We also took advantage of situations in select existing holdings to significantly increase our positions in Air Products (APD), Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Union Pacific (UNP). All represent high-quality franchises in attractive industries underwritten based on reasonable cash returns. It is an eclectic bunch, sourced from bottom-up analysis, not a trend-following, top-down approach.
  • Given our approach to investing — conservative, diversified and risk-averse — AI is not central to our portfolio. Indeed, no one sector or trend will ever be central to the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy.
  • In momentum-driven, concentrated markets, diversification inherently impacts relative performance comparisons. Of course, it also limits losses when that trade, inevitably, unwinds.

Outlook

  • We anticipate the AI debate will carry over in 2026, and we will continue to participate in AI in a measured and disciplined way. Yet, as other investors continue to myopically focus on AI, we expect to find additional idiosyncratic opportunities in overlooked corners of the market. We believe these seeds will bear fruit in the years to come. The Strategy, meanwhile, continues to trade at a significant discount to the broader market while exhibiting robust dividend growth on average. With market concentration and valuations at or near all-time highs, we remain committed to diversification and valuation discipline. These principles have served investors well in the past; we believe they will continue to serve us well in the years ahead.

data.title: You're Up 150% On Planet Labs, What To Do Now?
data.description:
Thesis

Planet Labs PBC (PL) delivered a particularly strong 4Q26 beat, and the stock saw significant upside of about 25%. Revenue came in at $86.8 million, a figure that exceeded expectations by about $8.7 million, while the non-GAAP EPS was $0.00, again beating the consensus by $0.05. So there is clearly accelerating demand momentum here. You can also see that in the guidance, 1Q27, the company expects $87 million to $91 million in revenue with an adjusted EBITDA loss of just $3 million to $6 million. The FY27 guidance is calling for $415 million to $440 million in revenue but only $0 to $10 million in EBITDA.

Now, since first initiating my coverage on Planet, shares are up about 162%. So a lot of investors are thinking about whether they should take profits. This is especially true when you consider just how aggressively this stock is now valued. Therefore, going forward, the question to answer is, can Planet justify a valuation that already prices in major success? Personally, I've been quite bullish on the whole satellite surveillance industry, favoring companies that first have the satellite coverage, but specifically those that are innovative in the space. Now, that innovative side could come from improving techniques like revisit rates and resolution or from AI integration. And in terms of building a diversified satellite portfolio, in which different clients can choose satellite coverage based on their needs, Planet is certainly leading the race. So in the longer term, I definitely see more upside to the stock and the industry as a whole. However, in the short term, investors should be aware of just how big the new expectations are on Planet and that we could see more short-term volatility.

FY26 overview

It’s safe to say that we saw some pretty strong top-line expansion from Planet. We also saw a meaningful shift toward operational maturity. Revenue growth was quite robust, up about 26% year-over-year to $307.7 million, with an especially strong figure for 4Q26 at $86.8 million. I simply see this as evidence of accelerating demand for their satellite data and analytics services. Now, what's even more telling is the surge in contracted future business. Their remaining performance obligations more than doubled to a hefty $852 million, and the backlog surpassed $900 million. That backlog figure is up about 79% since last year. So clearly, there is some high revenue visibility and sustained demand. I particularly saw this coming from government and defence customers such as Sweden, NATO, and U.S. defence agencies. If you think about it, the fact that 98% of annual contract value is recurring just goes to show how durable Planet’s business model is here. I’ve always viewed it more as a subscription data provider than a traditional aerospace company.

In terms of profitability level, the picture is a bit more nuanced but still very much improving under the surface. That being said, on the surface, we can see a pretty large GAAP net loss of $246.9 million for the year, but we have to keep in mind that this was heavily distorted by non-cash charges. Specifically, there was about $161.4 million tied to the fair value revaluation of warrant liabilities due to stock price movements. So if we strip these out, Planet would then achieve a key milestone: positive adjusted EBITDA of about $15.5 million for FY26. This is actually one of the main reasons I would remain buy-rated on Planet in the long term, in that profitability is getting closer. We can also compare this adjusted EBITDA to a loss in the prior year, along with $52.9 million in free cash flow and $134 million in operating cash flow. So what simply tells me is that the core business is now cash-generative.

As for margins, these are still somewhat pressured, and if you look across the surveillance industry of those using subscription services, margins have traditionally been an issue. Planet’s gross margin is slightly declining at 56% vs. 57% last year, which would indicate rising costs. There was an increase in operating expenses of $83 million QoQ. If you understand the nature of the business, I think this is inevitable due to just how costly satellite deployment is. That being said, the company still ended with a significantly strengthened balance sheet. They now hold about $640 million in cash and short-term investments, a figure that is up 188% year over year, and this should give them ample flexibility to invest. I see the main investment areas in CY26 being AI and next-generation satellite systems. I’ve covered their innovative side in my previous coverage.

So, for specific growth vectors here, it's now clear Planet is doubling down in two major areas: sovereign/defence satellite services and AI-driven analytics. We saw the expansion of large, multi-year government contracts and partnerships, including that notable agreement with Sweden and ongoing work with NATO and U.S. agencies. So they want to go after higher-value, long-duration deals. But at the same time, management is also setting up a nice position in AI as the next growth engine. The goal here is to extract more value from its already pretty vast Earth observation dataset. In terms of guidance for FY27, it is pretty ambitious, with revenue expected to fall between $415 million and $440 million, so again, continued high growth, but also near-term profitability pressure, since EBITDA is expected to be roughly breakeven.

Path to profitability

So this earnings was definitely an inflexion point. We’re seeing the high growth now get complemented by some emerging profitability and good forward visibility with that backlog. The numbers also indicate that the core business is beginning to scale efficiently. As per management, this whole progress journey is being captured in its achievement of the Rule of 40 in 4Q26. Now, this is a key SaaS metric where revenue growth plus EBITDA margin should be greater than or equal to 40%, which then indicates a healthy balance between growth and profitability. So on a full-year basis, Planet managed to reach a Rule of 30 a lot earlier than expected. Below is from CEO William Marshall.

“Q4 was also a record for revenue, representing 41% year-over-year growth and our fifth consecutive quarter of adjusted EBITDA profitability. For the second sequential quarter, we achieved the Rule of 40, which is revenue growth plus adjusted EBITDA margin. And on an annual basis, we achieved a Rule of 30 in a full year earlier than we anticipated. End-of-period backlog was over $900 million, approximately 79% growth year-on-year, providing us with excellent visibility to accelerate our revenue growth for the coming fiscal year.”

At the same time, the company’s forward visibility is what the market is not so bullish on. It strengthened dramatically, driven mainly by a surge in contracted demand. That backlog exceeded $900 million, up about 79%, and their remaining performance obligations hit $852 million, up about 106%. And keep in mind that about 65% expected to convert into revenue within 24 months, which is what we heard from CFO Ashley Johnson on the call, as you can see below.

“Our focus remains on managing the business to enable sustainable cash flow generation through efficient growth across our data solutions and Satellite Services revenue streams. At the end of FY '26, our Remaining Performance Obligations, or RPOs were approximately $852.4 million, up about 106% year-over-year, of which approximately 34% apply to the next 12 months and 65% to the next 24 months.”

The other good news is that the backlog expansion is purely sovereign satellite services deals, but also some growth in data and AI-driven solutions. The CFO also mentioned on the call that a lot of their "backlog growth is in data and solutions. In fact, that part of the backlog has almost doubled year-over-year."

Now, that's pretty significant because these offerings tend to be a bit higher-margin but also recurring since once a client gets a taste of the AI services and integrates them, it can be pretty hard to switch. It’s also far more scalable since Planet integrates AI capabilities that specifically allow customers to extract insights from the imagery and then deploy use cases faster. So that should be driving both expansion within their existing accounts and also new adoption.

However, the slight downside was that despite the strong visibility, management also embedded some degree of conservatism in the FY27 guidance. I think we can put this down to the lumpy and timing-sensitive nature of the larger government and satellite services contracts. This has actually been a recurring theme for a lot of small-cap defence players, where revenue recognition depends on milestone delivery and procurement schedules. So as a result, the timing of large new deal signings can materially impact results. Therefore, it means that Planet is assuming some deals will close later in the year, which then leaves room for upside if execution or contract timing comes in earlier than expected.

Valuation extremely stretched

When it comes to the valuation, the market is clearly pricing in some hefty future growth here. And what hasn't helped was the big 25% run-up post earnings. Now, despite generating $307.7 million in FY26 revenue, the company still trades at extremely elevated multiples with a 33.8x trailing sales and 26.9x forward sales multiple. Both of which are far above the sector median of roughly 2x. Likewise, the enterprise value to sales is sitting at 36.9x trailing and 26.5x forward, again going to show just how aggressively the stock is valued relative to its current scale.

I think the market seems to be underwriting a long-term thesis here with the focus mainly on high-margin, recurring geospatial data and analytics at scale. They hold an $11.5 billion market cap and pretty decent backlog visibility, so investors are essentially pricing in sustained 20% to 30% revenue growth with significant operating leverage going forward. This would also align with their remaining performance obligations of about $852.4 million, being up over 100% year-over-year, of which we can expect 34% to apply to the next 12 months and 65% to the next 24 months, as I mentioned earlier. Investors are also expecting a transition toward profitability over time. In that sense, the premium here would suggest expectations that Planet can evolve into a category-defining data platform, and I have to agree with them. It could be potentially akin to a space-based SaaS model, in which we see margins expand meaningfully as their satellite infrastructure scales. AI-driven insights would be another differentiating factor to increase monetisation. However, this also leaves very little room for execution missteps, and as the current multiple already embeds a very optimistic view of future growth, buying in at the current levels in the mid-$33 range may not be the best. In recent times, this has been quite volatile, so keep that in mind before buying in for long-term upside.

Just for a quick peer group comparison, Planet’s valuation is even pretty extreme among space-based SaaS peers. For example, BlackSky Technology Inc. (BKSY) trades at a forward EV to sales of 7.98x and a forward price to sales of 7.34x, compared with Planet’s 26.5x forward EV to sales. So roughly a 3x premium. Both are beginning to operate subscription-like satellite data models with the integration of AI for decision-making, but I feel Planet’s larger scale and multi-year government contracts are driving the market to price in a potential category-defining platform, similar to a space-based SaaS analogy here.

Key risks to keep in mind

So, beyond just the high valuation concerns, execution risk is going to come into play here. Planet now needs to consistently convert its $900 million backlog into revenue on time, sustain 20% to 30% growth, and also successfully scale/integrate those higher-margin AI analytics. It's especially important given that Planet relies on large, complex government and sovereign contracts. As you know, these deals are often milestone-based and subject to lengthy procurement cycles. They can also be slightly vulnerable to political changes. Even if we see minor delays or timing mismatches, revenue recognition could get a little lumpy. Now, usually this wouldn't be too much of a concern with a growing company, but due to the market’s high expectations for consistent growth here, it could have an impact on investor sentiment. This is especially true for a stock now priced for near-flawless execution; this kind of variability could possibly trigger outsized downside reactions.

Furthermore, to touch on profitability again, even though the company has recently shown us those early signs, margins remain thin and not that firmly established yet. So in that sense, there is little cushion if revenue growth moderates. Any deceleration here, whether due to macroeconomic pressure, customer budget constraints, or just a slower-than-expected deal conversion, could lead to multiple compressions, again given the stock’s premium valuation. Overall, the business is very capital-intensive. We're seeing a significant ongoing investment to expand satellite infrastructure and continue advancing AI capabilities. And whilst these investments are intended to support their long-term scalability and margin expansion, they also inevitably introduce some degree of near-term financial pressure. So adoption of these technologies has to be strong going forward and not lag expectations.

Finally, just from a technical and sentiment perspective, the stock has had that significant run-up, up about 162% since initial coverage and an additional 25% following 4Q26 earnings, which of course raises the risk of profit-taking.

Looking ahead

I think the main question we all have after these earnings is whether Planet can actually justify a valuation that already prices in major success. It should ultimately come down to execution against a very demanding set of expectations from the market, even more so after the run-up. On one hand, the bull case would argue that the current valuation, even though it’s appearing extreme at 27x forward sales and 37x trailing EV to sales on about $308M in FY26 revenue, is essentially underwriting a future where Planet scales into a multi-billion-dollar, high-margin data platform. That would also make up much of the long-term buy case here and leans pretty heavily on the $900 million backlog and guidance for $415 million to $440 million revenue in FY27. So about 39% growth, which would suggest sustained demand, particularly from defence and sovereign clients. Now, if we do see Planet maintain growth of 20% to 30% and expand margins via their software/AI monetisation innovations, today’s valuation could compress naturally as the fundamentals catch up. Particularly given that profitability seems to be just around the corner.

On the other hand, the bear case seems to be bringing us back to reality in that the market is already pricing in much of this success. Even if we use forward estimates, the company trades at a hefty premium to peers, while EBITDA is only expected to be $0 to $10 million in FY27. So would that then imply minimal near-term profitability? What that does is create a narrow margin for error, in that any slowdown in growth or delays in large government contracts, like we just saw, could trigger significant multiple compression. This is especially true when you add in the natural volatility of a small defence player. If you remember, back in January, this stock was nearing the $30 mark before pulling back to $20.

Furthermore, the backlog is still only about 2x to 3x annual revenue, so the company must continuously replenish these large deals to sustain the trajectory. So essentially, the stock reflects a belief that Planet will successfully transition from a capital-intensive satellite operator into a scalable, high-margin data/AI platform, but execution doesn’t just need to be good; it needs to be consistently strong over multiple years to validate the current price. However, I remain buy-rated on Planet with a longer outlook, since I see it evolving more into a category-defining space-based data and AI platform, with that upside coming from sustained 20% to 30% growth and, importantly, expanding margins. In that sense, I feel there's further long-term upside despite the near-term volatility we may see.
